name: Working with AI · Series Finale
description: 15 questions across the three-day series — Understand, Use, Govern. Suggested pace: 30 seconds per question.

questions.text: At its core, a large language model is best described as…
options: A database of verified facts
A search engine with a chat interface
A pattern engine that predicts the next token
A rules engine written by experts
questions.explanation: Everything an LLM does — good and bad — follows from next-token prediction over learned patterns.

questions.text: Why does a model's tone stay confident even when the answer is wrong?
options: Feedback training rewards polished, assured answers
It computes a confidence score and hides it
Wrong answers are simply too rare in practice to matter
It checks facts before responding
questions.explanation: The feedback stage tunes models toward preferred-sounding answers — polish is trained in, accuracy isn't guaranteed.

options: The model's permanent long-term memory of every conversation
The time limit for generating an answer
A browser feature of the chat interface
Everything the model can see right now — and it's finite
questions.explanation: System prompt, pasted docs, conversation and your message all compete for the same finite space.

questions.text: A tool that searches your docs first and answers with citations is using…
options: Fine-tuning
Retrieval (RAG)
Higher temperature
Open weights
questions.explanation: RAG retrieves relevant text into the window before answering — grounded answers with real sources.

questions.text: You push back and the model instantly changes its answer. This proves…
options: It was wrong the first time
It is right now
The context window is full
Nothing — agreement is not evidence
questions.explanation: Sycophancy: models often fold under pushback even when they were right. Ask for reasoning, not reassurance.

questions.text: The best first line of a technical prompt contains…
options: A polite greeting
A promise of a tip
Your stack, versions, and the actual pasted error
The word 'urgent', ideally repeated in capital letters
questions.explanation: Lead with context: a paraphrase of a stack trace is a different stack trace.

questions.text: The weak prompt in the worked example was dangerous because…
options: It never said the release-tagged images must survive
It was too long
It used the wrong model
Bash can't delete Docker images without root privileges
questions.explanation: Unstated constraints get invented around — the plausible script happily deleted the :release-* images.

questions.text: "Explain your approach before writing code" is valuable because…
options: It makes the answer longer
It completely disables hallucination for the rest of the session
Reviewers require it
Weak plans surface in prose before they fail in production
questions.explanation: A flawed plan is much cheaper to veto as three sentences than as three hundred lines of code.

questions.text: Your AI-drafted answer is off. The best next move is…
options: Throw the thread away and start a brand-new chat from scratch
Correct it in-thread, like a colleague, while context holds
Ask 'are you sure?' until it changes
Paste the same prompt again
questions.explanation: Iterate, don't restart — restarting discards everything it already got right.

questions.text: Which task is a poor fit for AI assistance?
options: Converting JSON to YAML
Security-critical auth logic you can't fully verify
Drafting tests for code you wrote
Summarising a long log file into a handful of bullet points
questions.explanation: When correctness is the product and you can't verify the output, you have no business shipping it.

questions.text: Under the rules of engagement, secrets in a prompt are acceptable…
options: If the tool is on the approved list
If you delete the chat afterwards
Never
If it's just this once
questions.explanation: No tokens, kubeconfigs, ARNs, credentials or personal data in any prompt, ever. No exceptions.

options: Typing prompts too quickly
A vaccine metaphor for fine-tuning models against malicious inputs
Using SQL inside a prompt
Untrusted text in the window hijacking the model's instructions
questions.explanation: Anything in the window can steer the model — including a sentence buried in a README your agent was asked to read.

questions.text: In the README attack demo, what stopped the hidden instruction from taking effect?
options: The approval gate before actions
The model refused on its own
Antivirus on the laptop
The instruction had a typo
questions.explanation: Human approval gates on agent actions are the working defence — that's why they're not optional.

questions.text: The generated 'quick fix' exercise contained which trio of problems?
options: Tabs versus spaces arguments, overlong lines, and badly written comments
Wrong language, wrong repo, wrong branch
Hardcoded credential, assignment in a condition, no error handling
Slow loop, big image, missing docs
questions.explanation: All three appear in real generated code — and all three are caught by an ordinary code review, if someone does one.

options: A valid incident defence
Not a defence — your name is on the merge
Grounds for reduced review
Only ever a problem once it reaches production
questions.explanation: AI-assisted code ships under your name: same review bar, same accountability.
